Different clients in Federated Learning (FL) exhibit heterogeneity in terms of resource constraints and data distributions, which impacts the overall performance of the global model.
Low-capability clients may struggle to complete local training in FL, leading to performance issues in the aggregated global model.
The distributed nature of FL leads to variations in data distributions among participant clients, causing domain shifts and affecting model aggregation.
Domain shifts occur in FL when participant clients collect data from diverse domains, leading to non-IID data distributions and biased global model aggregation.
Devices with stringent resource constraints may fail to complete model updates within the allowed time, resulting in exclusion from the FL process and missing out on valuable data features.
Devices collecting data from different domains may have diverse local models in FL, impacting the global model aggregation and potentially limiting the benefits for certain devices.
Existing FL frameworks aim to address system heterogeneity by compressing local models for heterogeneous clients, allowing the inclusion of low-capability clients in the FL process.
Various studies have focused on addressing challenges in FL, such as system heterogeneity and domain shifts, through the use of different FL frameworks.
The FL process involves working with clients' data to create compressed model tailored for each individual client.
Compressed models are created based on local data from clients, which can lead to over-fitting on their local domains.
Compressed models are susceptible to over-fitting due to the presence of domain shifts in the data.
The central server struggles to aggregate a robust global model with strong Domain Generalization capabilities due to domain shifts in clients' data.
Existing solutions explore domain-invariant learning approaches to address the domain shifts challenge in Federated Learning.
Existing solutions neglect the resource consumption of clients, making them unsuitable for direct application in heterogeneous Federated Learning.
The inherent nature of Federated Learning yields non-IID data across clients, which impedes learning a domain-invariant global model.
Existing studies inspired the proposal of DapperFL, an innovative Federated Learning framework designed to enhance model performance in heterogeneous environments.

The pruned local model is segmented into two parts, an encoder and a predictor.

The regularization term LDAR is constructed on the local objective.
The squared ℓ2 norm of the local representation encourages different local encoders to generate aligned robust representations adaptively, mitigating the impact of domain shifts.

The ℓ2 norm encourages each element of the representation to converge to 0 but not equal to 0, retaining more information. Compared to ℓ1 norm, ℓ2 norm does not make smaller representation elements exactly equal to 0.
Higher order regularization introduces significant computational overhead compared to ℓ2 norm, without significantly improving the regularization effectiveness.

Our proposed FL framework DapperFL is tailored for heterogeneous edge devices with the presence of domain shifts.
DapperFL integrates a dedicated MFP module and a DAR module to achieve domain generalization adaptively in heterogeneous FL.

The DAR module introduces a specialized regularization technique to implicitly encourage the pruned local models to learn robust local representations, thereby enhancing DapperFL’s overall performance, and achieve domain generalization adaptively in heterogeneous FL.

The MFP module addresses system heterogeneity challenges by shrinking the footprint of local models through personalized pruning decisions determined by both local and remaining domain knowledge, effectively mitigating limitations associated with system heterogeneity.
We designed a specific aggregation algorithm for DapperFL to aggregate heterogeneous models produced by the MFP module, aiming to realize a robust global model across multiple domains.
The evaluation of DapperFL’s implementation on a real-world FL platform demonstrated that it outperforms several SOTA FL frameworks on two benchmark datasets comprising various domains.
